message:
fix(fcp-store): count real canonical header size in quota (br-fm746)

Closes flywheel_connectors-fm746 (P2/MEDIUM).

Reviewer (cod_3 P4) flagged that MemoryObjectStore::object_size returned
`body.len() + 512` — a flat 512-byte estimate for header overhead
regardless of the actual canonical-CBOR header size. But ObjectHeader
carries variable-sized refs, foreign_refs, provenance, and placement,
and any canonically-encoded object up to 64 MiB (MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_
BYTES) is accepted by the structure validator. An attacker could store
many header-heavy objects with huge ref lists and tiny bodies — each
counted as ~512 bytes — and bypass max_bytes by orders of magnitude
while quota accounting still reported headroom.

Fix: object_size now calls StoredObject::canonical_bytes(&header, &body)
and uses the real encoding length as the quota charge. This is the same
encoding pipeline used for ObjectId derivation and for the 64 MiB cap
enforced by validate_structure, so quota accounting is now consistent
with the authoritative canonical size.

Fallibility: canonical_bytes can fail on non-canonicalizable headers
(NaN/Infinity/duplicate-key maps) or oversized payloads. put() runs
validate_structure first, so a failure in object_size indicates state
corruption — we fall back to a conservative 64 MiB ceiling rather than
panic. Quota overestimates are safe; underestimates are the bug.

Note: can't import fcp-cbor directly in lib code (it's a dev-dependency
to avoid pulling its transitive graph into every build), so the 64 MiB
fallback ceiling is a local constant with a provenance comment.

Test changes:
- New put_counts_canonical_header_in_quota regression: constructs an
  object with 512 ObjectId refs in the header (~17 KB canonical
  footprint) and empty body, then asserts (a) object_size > 4 KiB,
  (b) the new accounting dominates the old 512-byte estimate by >=8x,
  (c) max_bytes just below the actual cost rejects, (d) exact-fit
  accepts. This pins the attack surface.
- quota_boundary_exact_fit / quota_boundary_one_byte_short /
  quota_exact_boundary / storage_used_accumulates_mixed_sizes /
  put_quota_holds_under_concurrent_inserts: now size their
  max_bytes dynamically via MemoryObjectStore::object_size(&probe)
  rather than hardcoded 512-byte math, so they remain correct
  regardless of canonical-CBOR encoding changes.

Verified: cargo test -p fcp-store — 803 pass (802 baseline + 1 new
regression), same 2 pre-existing flaky quarantine::evict_expired*
failures. All 4 relevant quota tests (exact boundary, one-byte-short,
exact fit, put_counts_canonical_header) pass clean.

Cod_4 / SunnyMoose
